title: Evaluation of the Intersectoral Action Fund 2021-2022 to 2024-2025  - Canada.ca
content:
## Evaluation of the Intersectoral Action Fund 2021-2022 to 2024-2025
Final Report – Executive Summary
October 2025 Prepared by the Office of Audit and Evaluation Public Health Agency of Canada
Note: The complete evaluation report is available upon request. Please send an email to oae-bae@phac-aspc.gc.ca .
- Executive summary Background and Evaluation Scope Findings Design and delivery
- Background and Evaluation Scope
- Lessons learned Lesson learned 1 Lesson learned 2 Lesson learned 3 Lesson learned 4

### Background and Evaluation Scope
The Intersectoral Action Fund (ISAF) is a grant program that aims to improve population health and community resiliency and reduce health inequities through upstream actions on the social determinants of health (SDOH). Upstream actions focus on improving the systems and conditions that shape people's health, such as income, education, and housing.
Being the first program of its kind at the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC), the ISAF was introduced in 2021 in response to the health disparities that were exacerbated during the COVID-19 pandemic. The ISAF supports interventions at the upstream level that address the social, political, and economic factors that shape health and healthy behaviours. To do so, it engages intersectoral partners and diverse community members, including those with lived experience, throughout project's conception, design, governance, and implementation. Projects are expected to demonstrate inclusive governance, give attention to intersecting identities, and promote collaboration and mutual learning for all partners involved. After they document issues related to SDOH and potential solutions and actors, funded projects aim to raise awareness among health and non-health sectors about the crucial role they can play collaboratively to implement solutions.
From 2021-2022 to 2025-2026, the ISAF provided funding ranging between $25,000 and $250,000 to a total of 55 projects for either one or two-year periods. As of 2024, it has also funded the three territorial governments, through the Northern Wellness Approach, to implement upstream approaches. Grants were chosen as the funding mechanism to reduce the reporting burden on community-based organizations and maximize immediate impact. The ISAF is currently slated to end on March 31, 2026.
With its focus on intersectoral and upstream actions to address the SDOH, the ISAF aligns with the Agency's responsibility and priorities related to health promotion and chronic disease prevention.
ISAF stands out in Canada due to its dedicated focus on documenting needs in terms of intersectoral action and upstream interventions, potential solutions, and non-health players to address SDOH. Worldwide, only the United States has a similar program. However, domestically, a number of other programs or projects are funding interventions to address identified needs in specific areas of health promotion or chronic disease prevention. For example, PHAC's Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund (HCCF) covers downstream, midstream, and upstream interventions to enhance healthy living and the Mental Health of Black Canadians (MHBC) Fund integrates some aspects of upstream prevention but is targeted to support the needs of Black communities in Canada.
The Canadian Institutes of Health Research also have programs in these areas for which only academic researchers are eligible. Other federal departments have an important role in addressing the SDOH but there is a continued need to build further awareness on how their mandates have an impact on public health. Outside of the federal government, municipalities, provinces, universities and non-for-profit organizations are supporting the implementation of various intersectoral projects that focus on addressing upstream SDOH.

#### Achievement of outcomes
Funded projects documented specific SDOH issues to address, how and by whom. To do so, they had to build connections with non-health sectors. They strengthened collaborative structures and capacities, introduced mechanisms to support intersectoral actions and built trust across sectors that were not used to working together. The Tamarack Institute for Community Engagement (referred to as Tamarack) was also funded to support project capacity building and foster knowledge sharing through experience exchange and case studies. As a result of ISAF funding, funded projects developed new partnerships or enhanced existing ones and engaged with a broad range of stakeholders and sectors, helping them to understand community needs.
Overall, the program gained insight into how intersectoral collaborations can make progress in improving the SDOH. Based on this new knowledge, funded projects created a variety of documents for dissemination and mobilized new generated knowledge among actors, outside of the health sector, to raise awareness about how they could contribute to health outcomes and encourage them to take action. Most projects' reach was greater than expected among a broad range of equity-deserving populations and organizations. Produced documents will also remain available and are expected to sustain project efforts by providing a better understanding of the SDOH. As well, projects have put mechanisms in place to sustain their achievements overtime such as the following:
- establishing converging ways of working with intersectoral partners;
- developing trust and recognizing synergies;
- expanding partnerships, work scope or geographical coverage; and
- receiving additional funding from other sources.
Of note, assessing long-term improvements on the SDOH is not part of the evaluation's scope and cannot be done based on the current available data.
The ISAF's broad scope approach helped capture a variety of pressing SDOH issues in communities, alongside various organizations committed to potential actions to address them. However, this broad scope also resulted in the program receiving 1,300 proposals between 2021 and 2024, even though it had the ability to fund only 55 projects. However, only a low proportion of proposals met the upstream requirements (10% in 2021 and 23% in 2024) despite program efforts to provide additional clarification and resources to applicants. This highlights the high community demand for addressing SDOH and the need for more supportive, clearer, and more focused calls for proposals.
Overall, funding recipients and applicants were satisfied with most of the program design features, although opportunities remain around narrowing the application process, using plain language in application documents, simplifying the process to be more inclusive of smaller organizations; and setting funding timelines that allow for a greater impact. The geographical distribution of funded projects showed that some provinces, especially Quebec, had less coverage than others, relative to their population.
The initial one- and then two-year project funding was dictated by the pace at which the program was receiving its funding approval. However, some projects started before or continued after ISAF with funding received from other sources. Their experience showed that funding projects for three years instead of one or two would have helped them raise greater awareness among non-health decision makers and implement solutions with them. Reaching these last steps of the cycle was not possible within projects funding timeframe.
The program is scheduled to end on March 31, 2026. As such, this evaluation is providing lessons learned from the ISAF experience that could be useful for other programs in the future.

### Lesson learned 1: There may be an opportunity to explore how to integrate upstream interventions into existing programs.
While there are other PHAC programs that do some upstream work (e.g., the Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund), ISAF has a unique approach due to its explicit focus on community-based upstream action to address the SDOH. Upstream action also requires intersectionality, which was successfully integrated into the program.
Considering that the program is ending, there may be an opportunity to integrate considerations in advancing upstream action addressing the SDOH into other existing PHAC programs.

### Lesson learned 2: PHAC has an opportunity to define its role vis-à-vis other departments and determine what levers it can use to foster intersectoral collaboration in the context of its mandate and current capacity.
The SDOH touch on the mandate of multiple federal departments that have expertise and levers to address specific issues, but do not necessarily include a public health angle in their policies and programs. The ISAF's broad scope and approach was instrumental in understanding community needs better in relation to the SDOH, identifying potential contributors and actions needed to address them. Interdepartmental collaboration could help maximize effects by leveraging the resources and expertise of relevant departments. In this context, PHAC has an opportunity to assess what levers it has, beyond G&Cs funding, to foster progress in this area.

### Lesson learned 3: Ensuring that open solicitations for G&Cs funding clearly articulate eligibility requirements, especially for innovative programs with relatively new concepts, will help balance the efforts required from applicants and Agency staff. Using targeted solicitations or a narrower scope could be considered, as appropriate.
The ISAF received significantly more applications than it could support during its two open solicitation processes, and a relatively small proportion of these applications targeted upstream interventions. Submitting applications is time-consuming for applicants, especially those in small organizations, and significant involvement of federal public servants from multiple organizations is required to assess these proposals. In addition, more clarity is needed in defining and explaining with concrete examples the term 'upstream' in call for proposals documentation. Improvements implemented by the program in 2024 partially alleviated the process, but significant improvements are still needed.

### Lesson learned 4: Aligning expectations for results at the program level with the length of the projects funding period.
Ultimately, ISAF funding aimed to enhance system level factors that shape health behaviours and status among participants. While assessing results achieved for long-term outcomes was not part of the scope of this evaluation, evidence analyzed suggest that project funding which was granted on a one or two year basis was likely too short to allow projects to implement the full cycle of actions needed to foster improvements on SDOH. Aligning program expectations or goals to what is possible in terms of duration of project funding would help to better demonstrate results achieved.
